mnemonics.title: Cohen-Sutherland Outcode Bits
mnemonics.content: T R B L - Top(1000), Right(0100), Bottom(0010), Left(0001). Remember: TRBL sounds like 'trouble' - when trouble (outcode ≠ 0), you need to clip!

mnemonics.title: Liang-Barsky Inequality
mnemonics.content: p1 = x1-x2, p2 = x2-x1, p3 = y1-y2, p4 = y2-y1. Q values: q1 = x1-xmin, q2 = xmax-x1, q3 = y1-ymin, q4 = ymax-y1. Rhyme: 'P goes with difference, Q goes with window boundary'

mnemonics.title: Sutherland-Hodgman Steps
mnemonics.content: I S A K - Input vertices → Sort by clip edge → Add intersections → Keep inside vertices. Memory: 'I Saw A King' - the algorithm sees the polygon and keeps what's inside the king (clipping window)!

mnemonics.title: Weiler-Atherton Entry/Exit
mnemonics.content: E E - Entry goes in (keep), Exit goes out (clip). Think: 'Enter' starts with E, Exit starts with E. Both are E's - one enters polygon, one exits!

mnemonics.title: Trivial Accept/Reject
mnemonics.content: T A R - Trivial Accept when both outcodes = 0000, Trivial Reject when outcode1 AND outcode2 ≠ 0. Think: 'TAR' - Totally Acceptable Result vs Troubling Algorithm Rejection

mnemonics.title: Cohen-Sutherland Clipping Logic
mnemonics.content: A B C D - And then Bitwise OR, Clip at edge, Divide line. Algorithm: 'And' both outcodes for reject, 'Or' both for accept, otherwise 'Clip and Divide' - ABCD!

acronyms.usage: Used for line clipping when window is rectangular. Fast with outcodes for trivial acceptance/rejection.
Parametric line clipping, more efficient than Cohen-Sutherland for rectangular windows. Uses inequalities.
Polygon clipping algorithm that clips against each edge of the clipping window sequentially. Works for convex polygons.
Advanced polygon clipping that handles both convex and concave polygons. Uses entry/exit point selection.
4-bit code (T R B L) representing position of a point relative to clipping window boundaries.
When both endpoints of a line have outcode = 0000 (inside window), line is completely visible.
When logical AND of both outcodes ≠ 0, line is completely outside - reject immediately.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 2x2 grid around the window. Each quadrant gets a 4-bit code. Top-left = 1001 (Top+Left), Bottom-right = 0110 (Bottom+Right). Draw it like a tic-tac-toe grid with bits at corners!
Think of the line as a rubber band stretched from P1 (t=0) to P2 (t=1). The algorithm finds where the rubber band intersects window boundaries - those are your t values!
Picture a stamp with 4 sides (window edges). You push the polygon through each side - what's on the inner side stays, intersections are added as corners. Like cookie cutter!
Imagine walking along the subject polygon with a highlighter. When you hit the clipping boundary (entry), jump onto it and follow it, then jump back (exit) - creates new clipped polygon!
Picture a photo frame (window) and lines/polygons as objects behind it. Clipping reveals only what's visible through the frame - same as computer graphics display!

analogies.analogy: Like a bouncer checking ID at club entrance (clipping window). Each boundary (TOP/BOTTOM/LEFT/RIGHT) is a bouncer. Outcode shows which bouncers would reject the point - if both endpoints pass same bouncer, reject the whole line!

Like measuring how far a car can travel on a road before hitting boundaries. Parameter t (0 to 1) is distance traveled, p-values are direction, q-values are remaining distance to boundary. Calculate where car stops!
Like filtering water through multiple fine mesh screens. Each screen (window edge) catches (clips) part of the polygon. What's left after all screens is the final clipped polygon - pure and fitting!

Like using a stencil for spray painting. When polygon wraps around (concave), stencil method (S-H) fails. WA algorithm is like manually cutting along stencil edge, following both polygon and window boundaries!

Like sorting mail: If both sender and recipient are in same city (inside), deliver directly (accept). If both are in same foreign country outside delivery zone (same outside region), return to sender (reject). Otherwise, process further (clip)!
